Page 1

Bearing 315
Orienteering News for the Pacific Northwest
January, 1997

Coming Soon: Bids in for:

1997 USOF Convention; 1998 USOF Championships

Cascade OC will be hosting two Class A orienteering meets in the next two years. The first will be on August 23rd and 24th, 1997, at Trollhaugen, just east of Snoqualmie Pass, and will use the existing map (it will be updated this spring). Immediately following the A meet (on Monday and Tuesday), we will host the annual USOF convention at Camp Waskowitz near North Bend.

The 1997 A meet will be a "rehearsal" for the 1998 A meet, which we are bidding to be the 1998 US Championship. The 1998 date is firmly fixed as August 8th and 9th, as it fits between the North American Championships and other events in Kamloops, BC, the week before, and the World Masters Games in Portland the week after (see related articles). Under discussion is a change from the usual two day, total time format that the US championships has always used, to a one day individual championship followed by a relay event; the relay might be the US relay championships.

The 1998 meet will be on a new map in the Teanaway River valley, about 5 miles north of the town of Cle Elum and about 6 miles northwest of the existing Cle Elum Ridge map. Fieldchecking will take place during the summer of 1997.

The staff for these two events will be largely the same, with the goal of putting on a flawless event in 1998. Presently, the event staff includes Eric Bone, who will be in charge of the courses; Bill Cusworth, who will handle registrations; and Bruce McAlister. Rick Hood has helped with land owner permission (as has Ellensburg OC's Will Sperry), and Rick will be heading up the convention effort. As always, more volunteers are needed and welcome. Especially needed at this stage are workers for publicity and course design. Remember, early volunteers get a great choice of jobs.

If we do change formats as described above, then it would be possible for members to work one day and compete the next. However, it is my philosophy (and that of current staff members) that it is much more important that we put on a well organized and flawless event that is enjoyed by the participants, than it is to compromise the organization of the event so that the staff members have an opportunity to compete. It is not essential that the staff members be allowed to compete: the maps will be available for our use in the future, and there will be other championships. We look forward to hosting these two A meets, and we invite Cascade OC members to help us organize a wonderful pair of events.

Mike Schuh, Meet Director
home: 206-783-3960 schuh@farmdale.com

Inside this Issue:
Convention and USOF Champs Ski-O at Lake Wenatchee
Field Notes New COC Membership
Results and Winter O' Series COC Annual Meeting
* World Master Games at Portland
---

Ski-O Events

[Image: Skier Icon]

Lake Wenatchee
Now that the first ever St Edward Ski-O has been successfully completed, skiers can start waxing up their skis and look forward to Ski-Orienteering returning to Lake Wenatchee on Sunday, January 18. The map has been updated and buildings, roads and fixed trails located. Actual location of the ski-trails depends upon the trails groomed that weekend. If there is enough interest, we will try to rent a condo in the area for Saturday night. If you are interested in staying over Saturday night, please call Bruce McAlister at 206-821-6862.

If you drive, you will need a Sno-Park permit. Starts will be 11 to 1 in the upper parking lot.

Trollhaugen
A second Ski-O is planned for Trollhaugen late in February. The actual date will depend upon arrangements with the Sons of Norway. The tentative date is Sunday, February 23. Please check with the hot line for final dates and for overnight accomodations for Saturday night. Overnight users of the Sons of Norway lodge do not need a Sno-Park permit.

WIOL Inside
This issue of Bearing 315 is a joint issue of Bearing 315 and WIOL NEWS.

Copyright 1997, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering.
Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.

Page 2

Field Notes

Bullfrog Sold:
Don't miss the opportunity to run on the Sammamish Bullfrog Maps this year as the land has "leaped" to a new owner and the future of "O" there is uncertain.

New Trim Course
Cascade will be putting up a trim course in St. Edward State Park and taking a more active trail maintenance role in 97.

Northwest Intercollegiate
The "Pacific Northwest Intercollegiate" will be at Fire Mt. on March 1st. OK, maybe you are out of college, no problem, form an alumni or future alumni team. (Of course, we will keep this classes separate.) And wouldn't it be great, to have a UBC and some others BC alumni as well as from Oregon.

Skills Clinic
Speaking of March, Cascade and Husky OC are hosting a meet staff and skills clinic in later part of March. Like all "O" events this special event is open to all.

COC Updates
Eileen Breseman is doing a dynamite job with publicity, Don Denbo (a frequent course setter) joins the board, Rob Simpson signs on as volunteer coordinator, and "all-welcome" business meetings will be held on the second Sunday of the Month. (Call Ardis for meeting details at 364-4062.)

Service Recognition
COC would like to thank Margie Bone for serving as president for the past two years and Ron Brown for his service as volunteer coordinator. Both made a very valuable to the club and, in turn, to orienteering in the Pacific Northwest. Thanks again Margie and Ron!

The Big News
And finally the big news: In August on the 23rd and 24th, COC, with Mike Schuh at the helm will host a two-day "A" meet followed by a 2.5 day USOF Convention, with Rick Hood heading the effort. Mega details in the next issue, but if you want to make a suggestion or volunteer before then please call Mike at 783-3960 or Rick at 367-2606.

Rick
If you have items for Field Notes, call or send them to:
========= Rick Hood
19708 15th Ave NE #19
Seattle WA 98155
phone: 206 367 2606
email: rickhood@juno.com

World Master Games in Portland

The 1998 NIKE World Masters Games in Portland, Oregon will be the largest multi-sport participatory event EVER with 25,000 athletes expected in 25 sports, including Orienteering as a "core" sport! The Columbia River Orienteering Club will receive generous sponsorship including a new IOF map. However, our small O-Club needs some help from some of the more experienced orienteers from up north to develop a world-class event. If you'd like to get on board, please contact CROC's WMG Commissioner of Orienteering, Terradan Landchild <terradan@pacifier.com> (360)604-5263.

St Edward Score-O and Ski-O

Fifteen hardy participants braved snow and storm and successfully navigated the snowy roads to reach St Edward State Park for the annual Bog Slog/O Lang Syne, which this year also featured the first annual St Edward Ski-O. The lucky few were rewarded with brilliant sun and 15 inches of snow. In addition to some true COC diehards, the event also drew newcomer Scott Bleakley, Glen Cafferty who drove up from Portland, and Vladimir Gusiatnikov, who put his fate in the hands of the airlines and flew in from Cincinnatti. Of course, we told Vlad that this is just everyday weather in Seattle.

In spite of the snow, Meet Director Rick Hood had everything organised. Course setter Don Atkinson had set both a long and short option Score-O as well as a Ski-O course. All were vetted by Paul Magnus. Once on the course, everyone had a great time. Most elected to do the long option. Afterwards, all celebrated their victories at the COC annual pot-o-luck hosted by Bruce McAlister, joined by several who just came to party. A few remarked at the number of first place class awards until they learned that the winners were going to share their edible awards (home baked pies) with all. Rick Breseman also added to the end-of-year festivity by bringing...

Cascade Orienteering Club

Minutes of Annual Meeting

December 3, 1996
The meeting was called to order by Master of Ceremonies, Eric Bone, at 8pm at Cocos restaurant in Edmonds. Twenty members and several guests were in attendance. Outgoing President, Margie Bone, spoke briefly, recapping club activities and member involvement nationally. Ron Brown, outgoing Volunteer Coordinator, was presented with the Presidents Award. Ardis Dull received a Board of Directors Life Service Award. The following were elected unanimously to the Board of Directors: Bill Cusworth, Don Denbo, Paul Magnus, Debbie Newell and Jim Siscel. Eric reported on our 1998 U.S. Champs bid. Rick Hood reported on the 1997 "A" meet and U.S.O.F. Convention. The meeting was adjourned at 8:27pm.
Ardis Dull, Secretary.

Membership Report

It is that time of year again. When most thoughts are towards Christmas and family. The same is true for the membership coordinator. However it is also the time when COC must make an official counting of its membership for USOF. As of 12/15/1996 COC had 94 memberships total. Of these memberships, 53 were for individual members and 41 were for family/groups memberships. Under the USOF rules this gives the club an official membership of 135. This represents a loss of 16 memberships (26 by USOF Rules). Actual membership is probably greater then the numbers above indicate. There are many renewal notices out that had not been returned by the 15th of december.
Aaron the Alchemist

Page 3

COC Sustaining Membership Option for 1997

The COC board has elected to try, on a one-year trial basis, a Sustaining Membership Option linked to the calendar year. To take advantage of this option, you must mail the special form below, along with a check, in January. You can of course, join or renew your membership in the usual way (and at the usual price) at any time.

If, however, you elect to become a 1997 COC sustaining member, not only will you be helping the club; you will also receive some nice benefits. For example, you are entitled to attend all COC "C" and "B" sponsored meets for no charge in 1997. (Not only won't you need to bring your $4 to each meet, you won't have to print your name, address, phone, number etc, as there will be a special sustaining membership sign-in. You just sign your name and you are set to go.) Remember though, this applies only to COC meets and not to Sammamish, Nisqually, Ellensburg OC sponsored meets, for example.

Also, because sustaining members just "sign their name", registration for all should go quicker. Thus you are helping other club members as well as yourself.

Moreover, because your sustaining membership includes free meet registration, the total income at most meets will be reduced, and this works to the club's advantage as the club has to pay a percentage (10 - 20%) on top of a permit fee at most county and city park meets. This, in turn, helps COC keep "more of our money".

You might be interested to know that COC is slated to host 13 one-day "C" events, one clinic, and one two-day "B" meet; all of which are included in your sustaining membership. If one were to attend all these meets and buy a regular membership, costs would total $76. (Please note that COC's two-day "A" meet is not included in this sustaining membership package, nor is the sustaining membership transferable.)

(Because COC current youth fee is $3 and that is less the the $4 member rate for "C" and the fact that a family only receives one copy of the newsletter, the Board elected not to complicate the sustaining membership experiment with multiple family plans, for larger families, scout troops, or other groups.)

OK, that's the 1997 trial plan in a nutshell: here are some specifics.

If your current individual membership expires:
in December of 1996 or January, February, or March of 1997 your 1997 sustaining membership would cost __PH_0__ or in April, May, or June of 1997 it would be $51
or in July, August, or September of 1997 it would be __PH_2__ or in October or November, it would be $47

COC will also offer a two-adult family sustaining membership as follows:

If your current family membership expires:in
December of 1996 or January, February, or March of 1997 your 1997sustaining membership would cost __PH_0__ or in April, May, or June of 1997 it would be $101
or in July, August, or September of 1997 it would be __PH_2__ or in October or November of 1997 it would be $97

If you would like to help COC and become a sustaining member of COC in 1997, please fill out the following form, enclose the appropriate value check and mail to "COC Sustaining Membership" PO BOX 31375, Seattle, WA 98103 prior to January 31st. ( In order to have your Sustaining Membership recognized for the January 11 NAD Park Meet, we must receive your form and check by January 7th). For more information, please call Aaron at 935-8742 or Rick at 367-2606.

Regional Election

The Pacific Northwest Region of USOF will be electing a new Regional Representative and Alternate to the USOF Board by mail and at the Convention A meet. Nominations should be sent to Carl Moore. Bruce McAlister is the current representative and is not a candidate. Details later.

Travel Notes

Thinking of travelling to A-Meets this summer. You might want to check out the travel notes on the PNW O-Net. Late fares are listed there for out of town meets. Remember these fares change quickly.

Recent Round Trip fares have been: $131 to Kansas City for the Intercollegiates; $170 to Atlanta for the Georgia Navigator Cup and $186 to Columbus OH for the team trials. We are trying to arrange a group fare to Norway for the WC this summer.

SEE US IN THE NEWS AND ON THE INTERNET

Cascade Orienteering Club events are once again listed regularly in the following publications since October: Sports Etc., Seattle PI Getaways Recreation Calendar, Seattle Times Sports Calendar and Northwest Runner Magazine.
A press release about our local US Championship winners (run in Indiana/Ohio in October) was picked up by Sports Etc., Seattle PI and the Woodinville Weekly... yet more newsprint to our benefit.
If anyone has specific ideas or other suggestions as to where we might interest would-be, current and used-to-be orienteerers, please let me know. I'm hoping that regular long term exposure to the sports world will attract new folks interested in our sport.
Look for our new venture in January'97 on the Internet! We were recently invited to send our calendar and other press releases (including our Web site) to Microsoft's newly launched "Seattle Sidewalk" (http:\\www.seattle.sidewalk.com/). This online arts and entertainment guide for Seattle will be the first of the world cities to debut this service.
Eileen

Orienteers Surf the Web

Yes, the Northwest has not one, but two web pages. Check out our two web pages. Mike Schuh maintains one for COC at: http://www.halcyon.com/schuh/orienteering/coc.html. Howard Halter maintains "Pacific Northwest Orienteering Information" at http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html. Both pages have links to the PNW Schedule, and include driving help and site notes. In addition, Husky Club maintains pages on the UW pages.

The IOF maintains a web page at http://www.orienteering.org which has links to most national pages. The Orienteering Association of British Columbia also maintains a web page.

Page 7

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Newsletter

1996-97 Season -- Issue #1

Welcome to WIOL '96-'97

by Debbie Newell, WIOL Director

Welcome to a new season of scholastic orienteering events. I would also like to encourage you to get your friends and relatives to attend a meet or two. They don't have to be of school age. Old fogeys (like your parents) can participate in the annual "Winter O' Series," which runs concurrently with the WIOL meets. You wouldn't want them to miss the pleasure of running through mud, rain, snow, and underbrush, would you? Anyway, I hope you have a great time running through the woods, no matter what the weather is like.

Brief Updates

Inclement weather (try a foot of snow) made travel difficult for the St. Edward State Park meet in Bothell. Although the meet was not canceled, it will not count towards the season standings. Several people (though no school league participants) did turn up, enjoying the beautiful sunshine while running or skiing through the deep snow.

If you bought a WIOL season pass, you will get a $3 refund check. Also, you can still buy a season pass at the Lake Sammamish meet for $18.

It is always a good idea to call the Orienteering Hotline (206) 783-3866 for the latest updates.

Lastly, please visit our WIOL website hosted by Howard Halter at: www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/wiol/wiol.html

Running Smart

Tips by Eric Bone, former WIOL Champion and current USOF Team Member

When you're orienteering, you can sometimes get around the course pretty quickly without planning or paying close attention to what you're doing. Running without any plan or technique is a hit-and-miss way of orienteering: sometimes you will succeed, but often you will lose time making navigation mistakes. Two techniques that are helpful in approaching navigation problems are generalization and attack points.

Generalization: Orienteering maps are specially made with lots of detail. There are usually many more features shown on the map than you need to get from one control to the next. It is helpful to be able to "filter-out" the unnecessary information and only see features that are important in finding the control. This is not something that you do automatically, but takes practice. You can do this by looking briefly at a leg between two controls, then turning the map over and drawing on a sheet of paper all the features you remember which are important in finding the control. Look for the biggest, easiest features to find and ignore things that are subtle or don't affect your running or navigation. Trails, roads, and other linear features, big hills and valleys, and large thickets and open fields are all important in choosing and following a route.

Attack points: Some parts of an orienteering course are easier than others. It's usually easy to get near the control, but tricky to actually locate the control marker. You should adapt your orienteering technique to this by running fast in the easy sections and going slowly and carefully in the hard sections. One way of doing this is to use attack points. An attack point is an easy-to-find point from which the control can be approached, or "attacked." You should choose attack points which you are confident you can find easily and quickly, even if they are not on the shortest route--you usually lose more time than you gain by attempting direct, difficult routes when there are simpler choices available. Sometimes the attack point for a control is the control before it--when there's no easy intervening feature. Other times, the control is as easy a feature as any other; in other words, it is the attack point. These situations are unusual. Some typical attack points include a road bend, a stream junction, a trail intersection, the corner of an open field, and the top of a hill.

WIOL Results

WIOL Meet #1 -- December 21st
FT. STEILACOOM PARK
Nisqually Orienteers
article by Carl Moore, Meet Director and results by Don Denbo, WIOL Results Director

Many thanks to those who participated in the FS Meet. We had about 65 kids do the WIOL courses. About 20 ran the long advanced course and 8 on the short advanced. Special thanks go to Sid and Jan Whaley who helped with many tasks, Ken Peters who had the signs up early, Don Denbo who got up early and vetted the WIOL courses and suggested two small changes and hammered on the computer during the meet, Linda Hood who came early to do the WIOL registration (The first kid was on the HS course at 9:20 am!), Debbie N who helped with registration, Ellen, Lisa and the Whaleys for marker pickup.

Course Notes: I designed the WIOL HS course for a 30 min. winning time. A few kids were faster, but most of the kids were in the 30 to 50 minute range. Not bad. I made a major

Page 8

course-setting mistake on the HS course by making a last-minute change on their next-to-last control, from a small knoll to a fence around the northern water tower. (Paul Magnus sent me notes on past courses and I thought the knoll would be a little tough to find if I were to stick to my 30 min. guideline.) While setting out the controls I realized some of the younger HS kids might go to the wrong water tower where the middle school kids had a control. This in fact happened to 4 or 5 kids on the HS course, causing them to mis-punch. Too bad..........The long advanced course got mixed reviews from a few runners. The pit was chosen as a control site to get the mindless, non-thinkers to switch gears and speeds after several long, easy legs to some careful map-reading and messy going. I squeezed out a few minutes on this one, an "in-and-out" control from a small path accurately mapped. #13 in a small reentrant was placed to trap the fast runner toward the end of the course. Careful map-reading with some fierce berry patches not completely mapped made this one tough. I got an 8-minute delay out of one experienced runner! The course planner's sweet revenge! Controls were hung at ground level in some cases because we have a problem with stolen markers at this park. Remember last year? One crucial marker was stolen mid-way through the meet. It didn't happen this year.

Unsung heroes: Terry Bryant who drove the Bremerton bus and brought about 32 kids. Eric Bone volunteered to take the HS kids around the course for a critique. Steve Miner made a last-minute trip to the Xerox machine for description sheets that were running out. Jan Whaley brought the food and drink.

Thanks again for the response at Fort Steilacoom. Hope to see you at RAMBO in January.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 Orienteering News for the Pacific Northwest January, 1997

Cascade OC will be hosting two Class A orienteering meets in the next two years. The first will be on August 23rd and 24th, 1997, at Trollhaugen, just east of Snoqualmie Pass, and will use the existing map (it will be updated this spring). Immediately following the A meet (on Monday and Tuesday), we will host the annual USOF convention at Camp Waskowitz near North Bend.

The 1997 A meet will be a "rehearsal" for the 1998 A meet, which we are bidding to be the 1998 US Championship. The 1998 date is firmly fixed as August 8th and 9th, as it fits between the North American Championships and other events in Kamloops, BC, the week before, and the World Masters Games in Portland the week after (see related articles). Under discussion is a change from the usual two day, total time format that the US championships has always used, to a one day individual championship followed by a relay event; the relay might be the US relay championships.

The 1998 meet will be on a new map in the Teanaway River valley, about 5 miles north of the town of Cle Elum and about 6 miles northwest of the existing Cle Elum Ridge map. Fieldchecking will take place during the summer of 1997.

The staff for these two events will be largely the same, with the goal of putting on a flawless event in 1998. Presently, the event staff includes Eric Bone, who will be in charge of the courses; Bill Cusworth, who will handle registrations; and Bruce McAlister. Rick Hood has helped with land owner permission (as has Ellensburg OC's Will Sperry), and Rick will be heading up the convention effort. As always, more volunteers are needed and welcome. Especially needed at this stage are workers for publicity and course design. Remember, early volunteers get a great choice of jobs.

If we do change formats as described above, then it would be possible for members to work one day and compete the next. However, it is my philosophy (and that of current staff members) that it is much more important that we put on a well organized and flawless event that is enjoyed by the participants, than it is to compromise the organization of the event so that the staff members have an opportunity to compete. It is not essential that the staff members be allowed to compete: the maps will be available for our use in the future, and there will be other championships. We look forward to hosting these two A meets, and we invite Cascade OC members to help us organize a wonderful pair of events.
Mike Schuh, Meet Director home: 206-783-3960 schuh@farmdale.com
Inside this Issue: Convention and USOF Champs Ski-O at Lake Wenatchee Field Notes New COC Membership Results and Winter O' Series COC Annual Meeting World Master Games at Portland ---

Lake Wenatchee Now that the first ever St Edward Ski-O has been successfully completed, skiers can start waxing up their skis and look forward to Ski-Orienteering returning to Lake Wenatchee on Sunday, January 18. The map has been updated and buildings, roads and fixed trails located. Actual location of the ski-trails depends upon the trails groomed that weekend. If there is enough interest, we will try to rent a condo in the area for Saturday night. If you are interested in staying over Saturday night, please call Bruce McAlister at 206-821-6862.
If you drive, you will need a Sno-Park permit. Starts will be 11 to 1 in the upper parking lot.

Trollhaugen A second Ski-O is planned for Trollhaugen late in February. The actual date will depend upon arrangements with the Sons of Norway. The tentative date is Sunday, February 23. Please check with the hot line for final dates and for overnight accomodations for Saturday night. Overnight users of the Sons of Norway lodge do not need a Sno-Park permit.
WIOL Inside This issue of Bearing 315 is a joint issue of Bearing 315 and WIOL NEWS.
Copyright 1997, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.
Bullfrog Sold: Don't miss the opportunity to run on the Sammamish Bullfrog Maps this year as the land has "leaped" to a new owner and the future of "O" there is uncertain.
New Trim Course Cascade will be putting up a trim course in St. Edward State Park and taking a more active trail maintenance role in 97.

Northwest Intercollegiate The "Pacific Northwest Intercollegiate" will be at Fire Mt. on March 1st. OK, maybe you are out of college, no problem, form an alumni or future alumni team. (Of course, we will keep this classes separate.) And wouldn't it be great, to have a UBC and some others BC alumni as well as from Oregon.
Skills Clinic Speaking of March, Cascade and Husky OC are hosting a meet staff and skills clinic in later part of March. Like all "O" events this special event is open to all.

COC Updates Eileen Breseman is doing a dynamite job with publicity, Don Denbo (a frequent course setter) joins the board, Rob Simpson signs on as volunteer coordinator, and "all-welcome" business meetings will be held on the second Sunday of the Month. (Call Ardis for meeting details at 364-4062.)

Service Recognition COC would like to thank Margie Bone for serving as president for the past two years and Ron Brown for his service as volunteer coordinator. Both made a very valuable to the club and, in turn, to orienteering in the Pacific Northwest. Thanks again Margie and Ron!

The Big News And finally the big news: In August on the 23rd and 24th, COC, with Mike Schuh at the helm will host a two-day "A" meet followed by a 2.5 day USOF Convention, with Rick Hood heading the effort. Mega details in the next issue, but if you want to make a suggestion or volunteer before then please call Mike at 783-3960 or Rick at 367-2606.
Rick If you have items for Field Notes, call or send them to: ========= Rick Hood 19708 15th Ave NE #19 Seattle WA 98155 phone: 206 367 2606 email: rickhood@juno.com

The 1998 NIKE World Masters Games in Portland, Oregon will be the largest multi-sport participatory event EVER with 25,000 athletes expected in 25 sports, including Orienteering as a "core" sport! The Columbia River Orienteering Club will receive generous sponsorship including a new IOF map. However, our small O-Club needs some help from some of the more experienced orienteers from up north to develop a world-class event. If you'd like to get on board, please contact CROC's WMG Commissioner of Orienteering, Terradan Landchild <terradan@pacifier.com> (360)604-5263.

Fifteen hardy participants braved snow and storm and successfully navigated the snowy roads to reach St Edward State Park for the annual Bog Slog/O Lang Syne, which this year also featured the first annual St Edward Ski-O. The lucky few were rewarded with brilliant sun and 15 inches of snow. In addition to some true COC diehards, the event also drew newcomer Scott Bleakley, Glen Cafferty who drove up from Portland, and Vladimir Gusiatnikov, who put his fate in the hands of the airlines and flew in from Cincinnatti. Of course, we told Vlad that this is just everyday weather in Seattle.

In spite of the snow, Meet Director Rick Hood had everything organised. Course setter Don Atkinson had set both a long and short option Score-O as well as a Ski-O course. All were vetted by Paul Magnus. Once on the course, everyone had a great time. Most elected to do the long option. Afterwards, all celebrated their victories at the COC annual pot-o-luck hosted by Bruce McAlister, joined by several who just came to party. A few remarked at the number of first place class awards until they learned that the winners were going to share their edible awards (home baked pies) with all. Rick Breseman also added to the end-of-year festivity by bringing...

December 3, 1996 The meeting was called to order by Master of Ceremonies, Eric Bone, at 8pm at Cocos restaurant in Edmonds. Twenty members and several guests were in attendance. Outgoing President, Margie Bone, spoke briefly, recapping club activities and member involvement nationally. Ron Brown, outgoing Volunteer Coordinator, was presented with the Presidents Award. Ardis Dull received a Board of Directors Life Service Award. The following were elected unanimously to the Board of Directors: Bill Cusworth, Don Denbo, Paul Magnus, Debbie Newell and Jim Siscel. Eric reported on our 1998 U.S. Champs bid. Rick Hood reported on the 1997 "A" meet and U.S.O.F. Convention. The meeting was adjourned at 8:27pm. Ardis Dull, Secretary.

It is that time of year again. When most thoughts are towards Christmas and family. The same is true for the membership coordinator. However it is also the time when COC must make an official counting of its membership for USOF. As of 12/15/1996 COC had 94 memberships total. Of these memberships, 53 were for individual members and 41 were for family/groups memberships. Under the USOF rules this gives the club an official membership of 135. This represents a loss of 16 memberships (26 by USOF Rules). Actual membership is probably greater then the numbers above indicate. There are many renewal notices out that had not been returned by the 15th of december. Aaron the Alchemist

The COC board has elected to try, on a one-year trial basis, a Sustaining Membership Option linked to the calendar year. To take advantage of this option, you must mail the special form below, along with a check, in January. You can of course, join or renew your membership in the usual way (and at the usual price) at any time.

If, however, you elect to become a 1997 COC sustaining member, not only will you be helping the club; you will also receive some nice benefits. For example, you are entitled to attend all COC "C" and "B" sponsored meets for no charge in 1997. (Not only won't you need to bring your $4 to each meet, you won't have to print your name, address, phone, number etc, as there will be a special sustaining membership sign-in. You just sign your name and you are set to go.) Remember though, this applies only to COC meets and not to Sammamish, Nisqually, Ellensburg OC sponsored meets, for example.
Also, because sustaining members just "sign their name", registration for all should go quicker. Thus you are helping other club members as well as yourself.

Moreover, because your sustaining membership includes free meet registration, the total income at most meets will be reduced, and this works to the club's advantage as the club has to pay a percentage (10 - 20%) on top of a permit fee at most county and city park meets. This, in turn, helps COC keep "more of our money".

You might be interested to know that COC is slated to host 13 one-day "C" events, one clinic, and one two-day "B" meet; all of which are included in your sustaining membership. If one were to attend all these meets and buy a regular membership, costs would total $76. (Please note that COC's two-day "A" meet is not included in this sustaining membership package, nor is the sustaining membership transferable.)

(Because COC current youth fee is $3 and that is less the the $4 member rate for "C" and the fact that a family only receives one copy of the newsletter, the Board elected not to complicate the sustaining membership experiment with multiple family plans, for larger families, scout troops, or other groups.)
OK, that's the 1997 trial plan in a nutshell: here are some specifics.

If your current individual membership expires: in December of 1996 or January, February, or March of 1997 your 1997 sustaining membership would cost $ 53 or in April, May, or June of 1997 it would be $51 or in July, August, or September of 1997 it would be $49 or in October or November, it would be $47
COC will also offer a two-adult family sustaining membership as follows:

If your current family membership expires:in December of 1996 or January, February, or March of 1997 your 1997sustaining membership would cost $103 or in April, May, or June of 1997 it would be $101 or in July, August, or September of 1997 it would be $99 or in October or November of 1997 it would be $97

If you would like to help COC and become a sustaining member of COC in 1997, please fill out the following form, enclose the appropriate value check and mail to "COC Sustaining Membership" PO BOX 31375, Seattle, WA 98103 prior to January 31st. ( In order to have your Sustaining Membership recognized for the January 11 NAD Park Meet, we must receive your form and check by January 7th). For more information, please call Aaron at 935-8742 or Rick at 367-2606.

The Pacific Northwest Region of USOF will be electing a new Regional Representative and Alternate to the USOF Board by mail and at the Convention A meet. Nominations should be sent to Carl Moore. Bruce McAlister is the current representative and is not a candidate. Details later.

Thinking of travelling to A-Meets this summer. You might want to check out the travel notes on the PNW O-Net. Late fares are listed there for out of town meets. Remember these fares change quickly.

Recent Round Trip fares have been: $131 to Kansas City for the Intercollegiates; $170 to Atlanta for the Georgia Navigator Cup and $186 to Columbus OH for the team trials. We are trying to arrange a group fare to Norway for the WC this summer.
SEE US IN THE NEWS AND ON THE INTERNET

Cascade Orienteering Club events are once again listed regularly in the following publications since October: Sports Etc., Seattle PI Getaways Recreation Calendar, Seattle Times Sports Calendar and Northwest Runner Magazine. A press release about our local US Championship winners (run in Indiana/Ohio in October) was picked up by Sports Etc., Seattle PI and the Woodinville Weekly... yet more newsprint to our benefit. If anyone has specific ideas or other suggestions as to where we might interest would-be, current and used-to-be orienteerers, please let me know. I'm hoping that regular long term exposure to the sports world will attract new folks interested in our sport. Look for our new venture in January'97 on the Internet! We were recently invited to send our calendar and other press releases (including our Web site) to Microsoft's newly launched "Seattle Sidewalk" (http:\\www.seattle.sidewalk.com/). This online arts and entertainment guide for Seattle will be the first of the world cities to debut this service. Eileen

Yes, the Northwest has not one, but two web pages. Check out our two web pages. Mike Schuh maintains one for COC at: http://www.halcyon.com/schuh/orienteering/coc.html. Howard Halter maintains "Pacific Northwest Orienteering Information" at http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html. Both pages have links to the PNW Schedule, and include driving help and site notes. In addition, Husky Club maintains pages on the UW pages.
The IOF maintains a web page at http://www.orienteering.org which has links to most national pages. The Orienteering Association of British Columbia also maintains a web page.
The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Newsletter

Welcome to a new season of scholastic orienteering events. I would also like to encourage you to get your friends and relatives to attend a meet or two. They don't have to be of school age. Old fogeys (like your parents) can participate in the annual "Winter O' Series," which runs concurrently with the WIOL meets. You wouldn't want them to miss the pleasure of running through mud, rain, snow, and underbrush, would you? Anyway, I hope you have a great time running through the woods, no matter what the weather is like.

Inclement weather (try a foot of snow) made travel difficult for the St. Edward State Park meet in Bothell. Although the meet was not canceled, it will not count towards the season standings. Several people (though no school league participants) did turn up, enjoying the beautiful sunshine while running or skiing through the deep snow.
If you bought a WIOL season pass, you will get a $3 refund check. Also, you can still buy a season pass at the Lake Sammamish meet for $18.
It is always a good idea to call the Orienteering Hotline (206) 783-3866 for the latest updates.
Lastly, please visit our WIOL website hosted by Howard Halter at: www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/wiol/wiol.html
Tips by Eric Bone, former WIOL Champion and current USOF Team Member

When you're orienteering, you can sometimes get around the course pretty quickly without planning or paying close attention to what you're doing. Running without any plan or technique is a hit-and-miss way of orienteering: sometimes you will succeed, but often you will lose time making navigation mistakes. Two techniques that are helpful in approaching navigation problems are generalization and attack points.

Generalization: Orienteering maps are specially made with lots of detail. There are usually many more features shown on the map than you need to get from one control to the next. It is helpful to be able to "filter-out" the unnecessary information and only see features that are important in finding the control. This is not something that you do automatically, but takes practice. You can do this by looking briefly at a leg between two controls, then turning the map over and drawing on a sheet of paper all the features you remember which are important in finding the control. Look for the biggest, easiest features to find and ignore things that are subtle or don't affect your running or navigation. Trails, roads, and other linear features, big hills and valleys, and large thickets and open fields are all important in choosing and following a route.

Attack points: Some parts of an orienteering course are easier than others. It's usually easy to get near the control, but tricky to actually locate the control marker. You should adapt your orienteering technique to this by running fast in the easy sections and going slowly and carefully in the hard sections. One way of doing this is to use attack points. An attack point is an easy-to-find point from which the control can be approached, or "attacked." You should choose attack points which you are confident you can find easily and quickly, even if they are not on the shortest route--you usually lose more time than you gain by attempting direct, difficult routes when there are simpler choices available. Sometimes the attack point for a control is the control before it--when there's no easy intervening feature. Other times, the control is as easy a feature as any other; in other words, it is the attack point. These situations are unusual. Some typical attack points include a road bend, a stream junction, a trail intersection, the corner of an open field, and the top of a hill.
WIOL Meet #1 -- December 21st FT. STEILACOOM PARK Nisqually Orienteers article by Carl Moore, Meet Director and results by Don Denbo, WIOL Results Director

Many thanks to those who participated in the FS Meet. We had about 65 kids do the WIOL courses. About 20 ran the long advanced course and 8 on the short advanced. Special thanks go to Sid and Jan Whaley who helped with many tasks, Ken Peters who had the signs up early, Don Denbo who got up early and vetted the WIOL courses and suggested two small changes and hammered on the computer during the meet, Linda Hood who came early to do the WIOL registration (The first kid was on the HS course at 9:20 am!), Debbie N who helped with registration, Ellen, Lisa and the Whaleys for marker pickup.
Course Notes: I designed the WIOL HS course for a 30 min. winning time. A few kids were faster, but most of the kids were in the 30 to 50 minute range. Not bad. I made a major

course-setting mistake on the HS course by making a last-minute change on their next-to-last control, from a small knoll to a fence around the northern water tower. (Paul Magnus sent me notes on past courses and I thought the knoll would be a little tough to find if I were to stick to my 30 min. guideline.) While setting out the controls I realized some of the younger HS kids might go to the wrong water tower where the middle school kids had a control. This in fact happened to 4 or 5 kids on the HS course, causing them to mis-punch. Too bad..........The long advanced course got mixed reviews from a few runners. The pit was chosen as a control site to get the mindless, non-thinkers to switch gears and speeds after several long, easy legs to some careful map-reading and messy going. I squeezed out a few minutes on this one, an "in-and-out" control from a small path accurately mapped. #13 in a small reentrant was placed to trap the fast runner toward the end of the course. Careful map-reading with some fierce berry patches not completely mapped made this one tough. I got an 8-minute delay out of one experienced runner! The course planner's sweet revenge! Controls were hung at ground level in some cases because we have a problem with stolen markers at this park. Remember last year? One crucial marker was stolen mid-way through the meet. It didn't happen this year.

Unsung heroes: Terry Bryant who drove the Bremerton bus and brought about 32 kids. Eric Bone volunteered to take the HS kids around the course for a critique. Steve Miner made a last-minute trip to the Xerox machine for description sheets that were running out. Jan Whaley brought the food and drink.
Thanks again for the response at Fort Steilacoom. Hope to see you at RAMBO in January.
After One Meet at Fort Steilacoom

Code BETH - Bethel High School BETJ - Bethel Junior High School BREH - Bremerton High School BREJ - Bremerton Junior High School EVRT - Everett NJROTC HKEL - Helen Keller Elementary School KENJ - Kenmore Junior High School NTWS - Northwest School RIDJ - Ridgetop Junior High School SPMS - Saint Patrick Middle School STHS - Steilacoom High School TOJH - Thomas Jefferson High School

Time - Elapsed time on course IPE - Individual Points Earned TPE - Team Points Earned. For example, at the FS Meet, Rosalyn Pham of Bremerton came in first for women on course three and earned 100 individual points. She also placed 8th overall on the course and thus earned 86 points for her team in the team standings.
